title: Cat Vomiting Yellow Liquid: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Tips
meta_desc: Is your cat vomiting yellow liquid? Learn what bile-colored vomit means, when it becomes an emergency, and why photos help your vet with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Yellow liquid vomit in cats is typically bile indicating an empty stomach; while a single instance may be minor, you should seek veterinary care if it persists or is accompanied by lethargy and loss of appetite.

What is yellow liquid vomit in cats and why does it happen?

When a cat vomits a yellow, frothy liquid, it is typically bile. Bile is a digestive fluid produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder that helps break down food. Seeing yellow liquid usually indicates that your cat’s stomach is empty, as there is no food present to soak up the bile or change the color of the vomit. This can be caused by simple stomach irritation, hairballs, or more serious metabolic issues.

When is my cat's yellow liquid vomit considered a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for yellow liquid vomit is Medium. While a single episode with normal behavior may not be a crisis, you should contact your veterinarian right away if:

  • The vomiting occurs multiple times within a 24-hour period.
  • The vomiting is accompanied by lethargy or signs of hidden pain.
  • Your cat refuses to eat or stops using the litter box normally.
  • You need to rule out serious conditions like kidney disease, pancreatitis, or an intestinal blockage.

How does taking a photo of my cat's vomit help with the triage process?

Providing a photo of the vomit to your veterinary team is an excellent triage tool. Visual evidence helps the vet decide how quickly your cat needs to be seen by allowing them to:

  • Assess the exact shade, such as bright yellow versus brownish-yellow.
  • Evaluate the volume and consistency of the liquid.
  • Identify hidden details like small bits of foreign material, blades of grass, or traces of blood that might be missed in a verbal description.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Vomiting in cats can be caused by dietary indiscretion, parasitic or bacterial/viral gastroenteritis, anxiety, or motion sickness. Initial treatment involves withholding food for a short period, feeding an easily digested diet, and offering small amounts of water frequently. Careful monitoring for persistent vomiting, depression, abdominal discomfort, and/or fever is critical, warranting reevaluation if vomiting persists. Abdominal palpation should be performed to check for foreign bodies or abdominal discomfort, and careful examination of the oral cavity is important to look for yarn, thread, or needles. Abdominal radiographs should be performed to look for radiopaque foreign objects or signs of intestinal distention, indicating possible obstruction.

Chapter: Hepatology, Emergency, Internal Medicine

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 473)

faqs.question: Why is my cat's vomit yellow instead of clear?
faqs.answer: Yellow vomit indicates the presence of bile, which is released into the small intestine for digestion. If the stomach is empty, bile can back up into the stomach and cause irritation, leading to yellow-colored emesis.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat vomits yellow liquid?
faqs.answer: First, monitor your cat for other symptoms. If they seem bright and alert, you may try offering a small amount of water. If they continue to vomit or appear depressed, consult a vet immediately.

faqs.question: Can hunger cause a cat to vomit yellow bile?
faqs.answer: Yes, 'bilious vomiting syndrome' can occur when a cat goes too long without eating, causing bile to irritate the stomach lining. Feeding smaller, more frequent meals can sometimes help resolve this.
